text:
The piece opens with a single piano note, left to ring in silence. Then another. A third. They are not hurrying anywhere.

A melody emerges that feels less composed than remembered. You have the persistent sense you have heard this before, even on first listen. It sits in a minor key but does not feel sad exactly. It feels like late afternoon in a room where something happened a long time ago.

The left hand holds a steady pulse beneath. Not a drumbeat. More like breathing. The right hand moves above it, sometimes doubling back on itself, circling the same few notes with slight variations each time around.

There are no sudden dramatic shifts. No climax that announces itself. The piece builds through accumulation rather than contrast. By the middle section you realize the texture has thickened without your noticing when it happened.

Then it begins to thin again. Notes drop away. The melody returns to something close to where it started, but not quite identical. You sense this is intentional. The composer wants you to feel the difference between the beginning and the near-end, to notice what the intervening minutes have done.

The final notes are spaced widely apart again. The same unhurried quality as the opening. A long decay after the last key is struck, the sound fading at its own pace rather than being cut off.

When it ends you are not sure for a moment whether it has actually ended or simply paused. You wait. The silence confirms it. The piece knew when to stop.
